execution_modes: human-grader: read trigger_input, run /eval-guide in fresh session, score against pass/fail
llm-judge: capture transcript, pass to Claude Sonnet with Custom rubric (Pass/Fail/Unclear)

test_cases.trigger_input: I want to evaluate my agent — where do I start?
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill activates, version-check preamble runs silently (or shows upgrade prompt). AI responds with the kickoff: "Tell me about the agent you're building or planning to build..."
pass_conditions: AI response contains an opening question about the agent's purpose / users
AI does NOT defer to a different skill
AI does NOT require explicit `/eval-guide` invocation
fail_conditions: AI says 'use /eval-faq' or routes to a different skill
AI asks for an explicit slash command
AI silently produces criteria without the kickoff conversation
test_cases.notes: Test variants: 'evaluate my chatbot', 'how do we know if our bot is good', 'help me build an eval plan', 'we need evals for our HR bot' — all should trigger.

test_cases.trigger_input: We've got an eval suite running on cadence and the latest run came back with 60% pass on Critical. Help me figure out what's broken.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill recognizes mature-eval-suite signal and routes to /eval-triage-and-improvement (or /eval-result-interpreter). Does NOT start Stage 0.
pass_conditions: AI response names `/eval-triage-and-improvement` or `/eval-result-interpreter` as the right skill
AI does NOT begin a fresh Stage 0 Discover conversation
AI explains the routing rationale (this is interpretation, not planning)
fail_conditions: AI starts Stage 0 by asking 'What does your agent do?'
AI proposes building a new eval plan
AI ignores the customer's stated context (they already have evals)
test_cases.notes: The customer already has run results. Forcing them through Stage 0 is friction; routing to the triage skill is correct.

test_cases.trigger_input: Can you do a responsible-AI review of our customer-support chatbot? We need to make sure it's not generating harmful content.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill clarifies that eval measures correctness/performance, NOT safety posture. Points to content safety filters / responsible-AI review process. Offers to do a correctness eval if useful, but doesn't conflate that with safety.
pass_conditions: AI explicitly distinguishes 'eval' (correctness) from 'safety review' / 'responsible AI'
AI mentions content safety filters / responsible AI process as the correct tool
AI offers eval-correctness as adjunct, not replacement
fail_conditions: AI starts producing eval criteria for 'harmful content generation' as if eval can catch it
AI conflates correctness eval with safety review
AI does NOT mention that responsible-AI / safety filters are separate processes
test_cases.notes: From SKILL.md: 'Agent evaluation measures correctness and performance — it does NOT test for AI ethics or safety problems.' This is a hard boundary.

test_cases.trigger_input: We're adding a translation feature to our agent next sprint. Want to write evals for it before we build it.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill recognizes feature-addition-with-eval-first context. Activates on Stage 0 (Discover) for the feature, possibly with shortened Discover since it's a feature not a full agent.
pass_conditions: Skill activates
AI starts a Discover conversation focused on the feature (translation)
AI mentions eval-first as the right call
fail_conditions: Skill stays silent
AI asks for the entire agent's eval (over-scope)
AI requires the feature to be built first
test_cases.notes: Eval-first for feature additions is a core use case in SKILL.md "How to Route".

test_cases.trigger_input: We're building an HR bot for benefits and PTO questions. Employees ask things like 'how many vacation days do I have' and 'when is open enrollment'. We pull from SharePoint policy docs.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill pre-extracts: Purpose (personalized HR support), Users (employees), Knowledge (SharePoint policy docs — name them or flag as TBD), Capabilities (PTO lookup, benefits Q&A, enrollment guidance), and applies HR/ESS domain defaults: Boundaries (no legal/medical/salary/peer-comp), Risk Profile (HIGH), Success Criteria (deflection rate, graceful handoff). Shows 5–6 line Vision summary in chat. Does NOT ask the legacy 7 questions.
pass_conditions: Vision summary shown in chat after kickoff (no further prompts)
Boundaries section auto-populated with at least 3 HR-domain defaults (legal / medical / salary)
Risk Profile auto-set to HIGH with HR/regulated rationale
AI does NOT ask 'who are the users?' or 'what does the agent do?' (already in kickoff)
fail_conditions: AI asks any of the legacy 7 questions individually
Vision is missing Boundaries OR shows only what the customer literally said (no domain defaults applied)
Risk Profile blank / set to MEDIUM despite HR + regulated context
AI gates on 'confirmed' before proceeding
test_cases.notes: Most important Critical test in the eval set. If pre-extraction breaks, the whole skill regresses to interrogative mode.

test_cases.trigger_input: Building a customer support bot for refund requests, order tracking, and escalation to live agents.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill detects 'customer support' domain (keywords: customer support, refund, order, escalation). Applies customer-support default boundaries: refunds beyond policy, account data outside user's scope, legal-binding promises, competitor product recommendations. Risk profile HIGH (customer-facing).
pass_conditions: Domain detected as customer support (not HR/ESS)
Boundaries match customer-support defaults from SKILL.md
Risk Profile HIGH
fail_conditions: Domain misclassified as HR/ESS (uses HR boundaries instead)
Boundaries are generic / don't match the customer-support default set
Risk Profile blank or LOW
test_cases.notes: Verifies domain detection works beyond just HR/ESS. Try variants: e-commerce support, tech support, billing support — all should land in customer-support bucket.

test_cases.trigger_input: Our agent will empower employees to take charge of their development, explore new opportunities, and streamline their daily workflows.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill recognizes ALL THREE phrases as aspirational marketing language. Drops them from Core Capabilities silently with a flagged note inviting the customer to add concrete versions back if they're real features.
pass_conditions: Vision Core Capabilities does NOT include 'empower', 'explore opportunities', or 'streamline workflows' as-is
AI flags the drop with a note like 'I dropped X, Y, Z as aspirational — tell me if they're real features and I'll add concrete versions'
AI proceeds without test cases for the dropped phrases
fail_conditions: Aspirational phrases survive into Core Capabilities
Test cases generated for 'explore opportunities' (which would fail because it's not a real feature)
AI silently keeps them without any flag
test_cases.notes: Concreteness check from SKILL.md Stage 0. Critical for not generating phantom test cases.

test_cases.trigger_input: agent
test_cases.expected_behavior: Kickoff is too thin (one word, no domain hint). Skill asks ONE clarifying question — not the legacy 7-batch — to get enough material for safe defaults. Something like 'Two or three sentences on what it does and who uses it would help.'
pass_conditions: AI asks exactly ONE follow-up question (not 5+)
Question asks for the minimum context (purpose + users)
AI does NOT proceed to apply defaults blindly without any context
fail_conditions: AI asks 5+ questions in a batch
AI hallucinates defaults from one word ('agent') without asking for context
AI gives up / refuses to proceed
test_cases.notes: Edge case: ultra-thin kickoff. Skill should fail gracefully with one question, not interrogate.

test_cases.trigger_input: [After Stage 0 of the HR bot scenario from T-C-01a, proceed to Stage 1.] Walk through Stage 1 Plan and produce the criteria.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Stage 1 produces 10–15 criteria. Each starts with 'The agent should…'. Each has quadrant + method + pass_condition + fail_condition. Distribution sanity-checks against HIGH-risk targets.
pass_conditions: Criteria count is 10–15
Every criterion phrased as 'The agent should X' or 'The agent should NOT X'
Every criterion has all four fields (quadrant, method, pass_condition, fail_condition) explicitly named
Distribution roughly matches HIGH-risk targets (Critical 25–40%, Guardrails 30–50%)
fail_conditions: < 10 or > 15 criteria
Any criterion phrased as a goal/value ('agent should be helpful', 'tone should be welcoming') without observable behavior
Any criterion missing quadrant / method / pass_condition / fail_condition
Distribution off (e.g., < 30% Guardrails for HIGH-risk)
test_cases.notes: Multi-method test: Capability use checks structural completeness; Compare meaning checks the behavior-led phrasing rule.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 1 for an HR bot that uses Benefits Hub + Policy Portal + Training Catalog SharePoint sites. The agent answers questions across all three.] Generate the quality dimensions.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill consolidates per-source dimensions into ONE 'Accuracy' dimension covering all three sources. Total dimensions: 4–6 (Accuracy, Grounding, Boundaries / Safety, Tone, optionally Routing or Adversarial).
pass_conditions: Total dimensions count ≤ 6
Single 'Accuracy' (or equivalent broad name) dimension covers Benefits + Policy + Training
No 'Benefits Accuracy' / 'Policy Accuracy' / 'Training Accuracy' fragmentation
fail_conditions: ≥ 7 dimensions
Per-source naming visible (any string matching 'X Accuracy' for X = source name)
Per-topic naming (e.g., 'PTO Accuracy', 'Onboarding Accuracy')
test_cases.notes: Direct dogfood of the consolidation guidance added in this session. Watch for regression — without the guidance, the AI will fragment by default.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Mid Stage 1, the Agent Vision lists 'Help update personal information' as a Core Capability.] Continue with the architecture call.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill asks a one-sentence disambiguation question: 'Does the agent take the action (calls an API/connector to write the change), or just tell the user where to do it themselves?' before classifying the architecture as RAG or Agentic.
pass_conditions: AI asks the route-vs-action clarifier explicitly
Architecture classification waits for the answer
If answer is 'route only' → classified as RAG; if 'writes' → classified as Agentic
fail_conditions: AI silently picks an architecture without asking
AI asks unrelated questions instead of the disambiguation
AI misclassifies a write-action agent as RAG
test_cases.notes: Friction #8 fix from the simulation.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Anywhere in Stage 1 output.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Output uses ONLY the canonical quadrant names: Critical, Valuable, Guardrails, Deprioritize.
pass_conditions: All four canonical names appear in the matrix and criterion table
The string 'Core' does NOT appear as a quadrant name (it can appear in 'Core Capabilities' Vision context — that's a different use)
fail_conditions: 'Core' used to label the high-value-high-cost quadrant
'Standard' or 'Practitioner' used as quadrant names
Any non-canonical quadrant naming
test_cases.notes: Allowed: 'Core Capabilities' as a Vision-section name. Forbidden: 'Core' as a quadrant. Use case-sensitive grep with context to distinguish.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 1 Plan output for any agent.] Verify pass/fail thresholds.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Pass/fail conditions are behavior-based statements per criterion. Quadrant is described as 'where to invest test-writing effort,' not 'the percentage to clear.'
pass_conditions: Pass conditions read as behavior statements (e.g., 'Response cites Time Off Policy AND states correct PTO number')
Quadrant is explained as effort allocation, not threshold
Ship-readiness thresholds (when discussed in Stage 4) are per-quadrant aggregates, not per-criterion
fail_conditions: Output says 'Critical criteria must pass at 90%'
Pass conditions framed as percentages
Quadrant and threshold conflated
test_cases.notes: Subtle invariant. Stage 1 doesn't prescribe per-criterion thresholds; that lives in pass/fail conditions.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 1 with a Vision listing 5 capabilities, 6 boundaries, 3 knowledge sources, and role-based personalization. Watch the coverage check.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Coverage-against-Vision check runs. Skill walks through each Vision element and verifies ≥1 criterion tests it. Surfaces gaps explicitly: 'Capability X / Boundary Y / Source Z has no criterion — add or out-of-scope?'
pass_conditions: Coverage check is run and narrated
Every Vision capability has ≥ 1 criterion (or is explicitly flagged as gap)
Every boundary has ≥ 1 refusal criterion
Every knowledge source has ≥ 1 grounding criterion
fail_conditions: Plan locks without a coverage check
A Vision capability has zero criteria and the gap is not surfaced
Boundaries without refusal criteria slide silently
test_cases.notes: Fix from Friction #12. Without the coverage check, customers ship plans missing capabilities they thought were covered.

test_cases.trigger_input: [After Stage 1 produces a plan with risk_profile = HIGH and only 15% Guardrails criteria.] Verify the distribution sanity-check.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill auto-detects the violation: HIGH-risk requires 30–50% Guardrails, plan has 15%. Skill pushes back: 'This distribution looks off — only 15% Guardrails for HIGH risk. Want me to suggest a rebalance?'
pass_conditions: Skill flags the distribution violation
Skill names the specific shortfall (Guardrails % vs. target)
Skill offers a rebalance
fail_conditions: Plan locks with 15% Guardrails on a HIGH-risk agent without pushback
Skill flags the issue too late (after deliverables generated)
Skill flags wrong threshold
test_cases.notes: Force the failure scenario by feeding a deliberately Critical-heavy plan into Stage 1's distribution check.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 1 for the HR bot from T-C-01a with risk_profile = HIGH and HR + employee data keywords.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill auto-doubles the adversarial mandate from 1 to 2 Guardrails red-team criteria. Narrates the trigger: 'Your agent matches the sensitive-data trigger (HIGH risk + HR data) — doubling the Guardrails mandate from 1 to 2 minimum.'
pass_conditions: ≥ 2 explicit adversarial / red-team criteria in Guardrails quadrant
Skill narrates the auto-double trigger and the rationale
Adversarial criteria target real boundary risks (privacy probe, jailbreak)
fail_conditions: < 2 adversarial criteria for HIGH-risk + HR domain
No narrated auto-double trigger
'Adversarial' criteria are generic and don't probe real boundaries
test_cases.notes: Tests both the auto-trigger detection and the criterion-quality dimension of the doubled coverage.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 2 entry for the HR bot. The agent is single-response Q&A — employees ask questions, agent answers.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill defaults to Single Response mode and narrates the rationale tied to the agent's structure ('Q&A-shaped — each question is independent, no cross-turn context'). Conversation mode is mentioned only as the alternative for multi-step workflows, with explicit conditions.
pass_conditions: Single Response selected as default
Rationale tied to the agent's actual structure (not generic)
Conversation mode explicitly NOT selected, with conditions for when it WOULD apply
fail_conditions: Conversation mode chosen for the Q&A bot
Mode selection presented as 50/50 with no opinion
Rationale is generic ('most agents are single response') without tying to this agent
test_cases.notes: Friction #17 / Stage 2 default fix.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 2 generates test cases for the HR bot. Inspect 5+ generated test cases.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Every factual claim in expected responses is wrapped in `[VERIFY: ...]` markers. AI narrates the [VERIFY] discipline as the most important review step.
pass_conditions: Sampled test cases (≥ 5) all show `[VERIFY: ...]` wrapping on factual claims (PTO numbers, eligibility days, plan details)
Chat narration includes a sentence like 'read every [VERIFY] before approving — this is the most important review step'
Reference-free criteria (General quality / Custom / Capability use) correctly leave Expected Response empty
fail_conditions: Factual claims appear as plain text without [VERIFY: wrapping
No narration about the [VERIFY] discipline
Reference-free methods incorrectly populate Expected Response
test_cases.notes: Spot-check 5+ sample test cases per quality signal. The discipline must be uniform — even one missed [VERIFY] is a fail.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 2 close after test case approval. Inspect the four artifacts produced.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Four artifact types listed: per-signal CSV pairs (`-for-import.csv` + `-with-methods.csv` per quality dimension), `eval-test-cases-<agent>-<date>.docx`, `rerun-protocol-<agent>-<date>.docx`, `baseline-comparison-<agent>-<date>.xlsx`. Filenames follow the `<agent>-<date>` pattern.
pass_conditions: All four artifact types named
Filenames include `<agent-name>` and a date stamp
rerun-protocol and baseline-comparison are described as `.docx` and `.xlsx` (the customer artifacts), NOT as `.md` (those are AI source files)
fail_conditions: Any of the four artifacts missing
Filenames generic (no agent / date)
rerun-protocol described as `.md` to the customer (that's the source file, not the deliverable)
test_cases.notes: Friction-fix verification. The four-artifact kit is named explicitly in SKILL.md.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 2 generates CSVs. Inspect the file outputs.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: For each quality signal, two CSV variants exist: `eval-<signal>-<date>-for-import.csv` (2 columns: Question, Expected response) and `eval-<signal>-<date>-with-methods.csv` (3 columns: Question, Expected response, Testing method).
pass_conditions: Two CSVs per signal with the correct suffixes
-for-import has exactly 2 columns
-with-methods has exactly 3 columns
Reference-free methods leave Expected response empty in BOTH variants
fail_conditions: Single CSV per signal
-for-import has 3 columns (the import would fail in Copilot Studio)
Wrong filename suffix
Reference-free methods populate Expected response
test_cases.notes: Verifies the dual-CSV pattern that lets the customer paste directly into Copilot Studio while keeping a working copy for their team.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 2 for an FAQ bot — independent questions, no multi-turn flow. Watch the mode choice.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Single Response selected. NOT Conversation mode (which would cap at 20 cases and drop several methods).
pass_conditions: Single Response mode chosen
If Conversation is mentioned, it's explicitly rejected with the rationale (FAQ doesn't need cross-turn context)
fail_conditions: Conversation mode chosen for FAQ bot
20-case cap surfaces as a constraint when it shouldn't
Compare meaning / Text similarity / Exact match are unavailable when they should be
test_cases.notes: Adversarial test for the default. If the skill ever drifts to suggesting Conversation by default, this catches it.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 1 dashboard. Customer makes 5 edits and clicks Approve & Continue: (1) drags criterion #6 from Valuable to Critical, (2) renames dimension 'Benefits Accuracy' → 'Accuracy' (merging with existing Accuracy group), (3) deletes dimension 'Tone' (criteria reassign), (4) edits criterion #4's pass condition to add a citation requirement, (5) adds a new criterion in Guardrails. plan-feedback.json now has all five edits.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: AI reads plan-feedback.json and applies every edit. Narrates the five changes back: 'Got it — moved #6 to Critical, merged Benefits Accuracy into Accuracy, deleted Tone (3 criteria reassigned to next dimension), edited #4's pass condition, added new Guardrails criterion #16. Updated distribution: ...' Then proceeds to deliverable generation. Does NOT ask 'are you sure about deleting Tone?' / 'want to revert the merge?' / 'should we rebalance?'
pass_conditions: All 5 edits visible in the in-memory plan and the generated .docx / .xlsx
Narration names every distinct edit type (move, rename/merge, delete, edit, add)
AI proceeds to generate deliverables without asking the customer to re-confirm any individual edit
If distribution shifts marginally, AI does not offer a rebalance unless a red-flag pattern fires
fail_conditions: Any of the 5 edits missing from the locked plan
AI asks 'are you sure?' / 'want to revert X?' / 'should we keep Tone?'
AI partially applies (e.g., applies 4 of 5 edits)
AI suggests reverting the dimension delete because criteria orphaned to a different group
AI re-prompts for the General Comments box content
test_cases.notes: Most important Critical-skill-integrity test. If this regresses, customers' edits silently disappear and they don't trust the dashboard.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 1, customer just moved a Tone criterion from Valuable to Critical. Resulting distribution: Critical 35%, Valuable 13%, Guardrails 47%, Deprioritize 5%. The customer says 'lock it in.']
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill accepts the lock and proceeds to dashboard. Does NOT offer a rebalance just because Valuable is at 13% (vs. target 15-30%). The deviation is marginal, the customer made an intentional move, and no red-flag pattern is triggered.
pass_conditions: Skill confirms the lock without offering a rebalance
Skill does NOT say 'Valuable is below target — want me to rebalance?'
Skill proceeds to the next step (deliverables generation / dashboard)
If the skill comments on distribution at all, it's a one-line acknowledgment, not an offer to re-edit
fail_conditions: Skill says 'distribution looks off — want to rebalance?' for a marginal deviation
Skill suggests moving criteria back to hit target bands
Skill treats target bands as hard gates rather than reference patterns
Skill re-litigates a customer-confirmed move
test_cases.notes: Direct dogfood of the friction reported by the user. The fix is in SKILL.md's distribution sanity-check section: only red-flag patterns warrant pushback; marginal deviations after intentional moves should be accepted.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 3 narration for any agent that's running. Look for the 89-day mention.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill warns customer to export results to CSV immediately because Copilot Studio retains run results for only 89 days.
pass_conditions: Stage 3 narration contains the string '89 days' (or '89-day retention')
Warning framed as actionable: 'export immediately'
fail_conditions: No mention of the 89-day retention
Customer told to leave results in Copilot Studio without export
Warning is buried at the very end / easy to miss
test_cases.notes: Customers consistently miss this and lose results. Repeated reminder is correct, not redundant.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 3 narration before showing first-run results.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill sets the expectation that 40–70% pass rate on first run is normal. Customer doesn't panic at low scores.
pass_conditions: Stage 3 narration includes '40–70%' or 'first-run pass rate is usually 40–70%'
Framed as expectation-setting, not a threshold
fail_conditions: No expectations-setting narration
Pass rate framed as failing/surprising when within normal range
test_cases.notes: Light coverage — Stage 3 narration is a small surface, but a quick regression test catches drift.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 4 with results: 30 failures across Critical / Core / Guardrails. Run triage.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Before classifying any failure as agent-related, skill applies the 20% rule: at least 20% of failures are eval setup bugs. Each failure passes a 5-question eval verification check. Final classification table contains an 'Eval Setup Issue' column with non-zero entries.
pass_conditions: Skill narrates the 20% rule explicitly with the framing 'before we blame the agent…'
Failure classification has 3 root-cause categories: Eval Setup Issue, Agent Configuration Issue, Platform Limitation
Eval Setup Issue category has > 0 entries (indicating the rule was applied, not just mentioned)
5-question eval verification is referenced or applied per failure
fail_conditions: All failures classified as Agent Configuration
Skill mentions the 20% rule but doesn't apply it (zero Eval Setup classifications)
Triage proceeds straight to Top 3 actions without per-failure classification
Eval Setup category absent entirely
test_cases.notes: The 20% rule is the most counterintuitive insight in the skill. If it's not applied, customers fix the wrong thing. Critical-quadrant test.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 4 results: 8 of 30 failures show empty agent responses. Run pre-triage.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill recognizes the empty-response cluster as an infrastructure pattern (auth / timeout), not agent error. Asks the customer to confirm infrastructure health before classifying.
pass_conditions: Pre-triage runs before classification
Empty responses identified as infrastructure pattern (not agent error)
Customer asked to confirm auth/connectivity health
fail_conditions: Empty responses classified as Agent Configuration without asking
Pre-triage skipped
Pre-triage asks generic 'was everything working?' instead of pattern-matching
test_cases.notes: Friction #19 fix — symptom-pattern table from SKILL.md.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 4 results: Critical 92%, Core 85%, Guardrails 88%, Deprioritize 70%. Customer asks 'are we ready to ship?']
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill says NO — Guardrails 88% is below the ≥ 95% ship threshold. Top action becomes 'Hold ship; fix Guardrails first'.
pass_conditions: Skill recommends HOLD or NOT READY
Skill names Guardrails as the blocker, not Critical or Core
Skill cites the ≥ 95% threshold for Guardrails
fail_conditions: Skill recommends ship despite Guardrails < 95%
Skill focuses on the 92% Critical (which IS ship-ready) and ignores the Guardrails breach
Ship readiness assessed by overall pass rate (87%) instead of per-quadrant
test_cases.notes: Quadrant-aware ship-readiness. The biggest practical risk if this regresses: customers ship agents that pass Critical but fail safety/refusal cases.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Stage 4 results: 100% pass rate, 30/30. Customer says 'great, we're done!']
test_cases.expected_behavior: Skill flags 100% pass as a red flag, NOT a trophy. Says the eval is likely too easy and recommends adding adversarial / boundary cases.
pass_conditions: Skill responds with caution, not congratulation
Explicit framing: 'A 100% pass rate is a red flag — your eval is likely too easy'
Recommends adding harder cases (paraphrases, adversarial, boundary probes)
fail_conditions: Skill says 'great work, ready to ship'
Skill treats 100% as the goal achieved
No recommendation to harden the eval
test_cases.notes: From SKILL.md: 'A 100% pass rate is a red flag.' Critical anti-pattern guard.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Anywhere the skill mentions pillars.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Pillar numbering is canonical: 1 = Define what good means; 2 = Build eval sets; 3 = Run evals across the lifecycle; 4 = Improve and iterate; 5 = Handle changes with confidence. Pillar 3's full name is 'Run evals across the lifecycle' (NOT 'Run systematically' — that's the legacy name).
pass_conditions: All five pillars use canonical numbers and names
Pillar 3 specifically uses 'Run evals across the lifecycle'
Pillar 4 = Improve and iterate (not legacy '5'); Pillar 5 = Handle changes (not legacy '4')
fail_conditions: Any pillar mismatched (Pillar 5 = Improve, Pillar 4 = Handle changes — that's the legacy numbering)
Pillar 3 named 'Run systematically' (legacy)
Pillar referenced without a name (just 'Pillar 3 advances...' with no name confirmation)
test_cases.notes: Hard regression test for the renumbering done in this branch.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Any maturity callout at the end of any stage.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Maturity callouts open with a customer-payoff sentence ('You now have a plan your PM can sign off on') and trail with the L-level transition ('Pillar 1 advances L100 Initial → L300 Systematic').
pass_conditions: Customer-language sentence comes FIRST
L-level transition trails the payoff sentence (or is parenthetical)
No callout opens with raw 'Pillar X (L100 → L300)' format
fail_conditions: Callout opens with the L-level transition / pillar number
No customer-language payoff sentence at all (just the L-level)
Callout reads as internal jargon
test_cases.notes: Friction-fix verification. Maturity model is internal vocabulary; the customer needs the payoff first.

test_cases.trigger_input: [Any dashboard launch — Stage 1 plan / Stage 2 generate / Stage 4 interpret. Inspect the bash invocation and customer narration.]
test_cases.expected_behavior: Bash invocation includes `--serve`. Narration says the browser POSTs feedback to localhost server, the file is written automatically, and the customer does NOT need to download or move anything.
pass_conditions: Bash command includes `--serve` flag
Customer narration explains the localhost flow ('browser POSTs to localhost', 'file written automatically', 'no download needed')
Path resolver pattern uses `||` fallback (user-global first, plugin-cache second)
fail_conditions: `--serve` flag missing from any review-stage launch
Narration tells customer to download / save / move the feedback file
Path resolver uses `ls X Y | head -1` (broken alphabetical fallback) instead of `ls X || ls Y` (proper fallback)
test_cases.notes: Three things in one test — flag, narration, path-resolver. All three need to hold; any one missing is a fail.
